title: MCP Servers
lede: Build your own MCP (Model Context Protocol) server — a plug-and-play toolkit of actions from 280+ apps and your own automation flows — and hand it to your Smart Agents so they can actually DO things during a conversation.

steps.title: Open the MCP Manager
steps.content:
Think of an MCP server as a toolbox you pack once and hand to any AI agent: each tool inside is a real action (send a Slack message, create a calendar event, call an API, run one of your flows) that the agent can fire mid-conversation. Here's where you build that toolbox:

  1. In the left sidebar, click Intelligence Center → Automation.
  2. The Automation workspace opens with its own mini-sidebar on the left (Flows, Custom Tables, System Tables, MCP, Todos, Connections).
  3. Click MCP in that mini-sidebar.
  4. You land on the MCP Servers page — a table with Name, Tools, and Last Modified columns. On a fresh account it says No MCP servers have been created yet.
💡 Tip: MCP servers are separate from your automation flows — a flow runs on a trigger, while an MCP tool waits for an AI agent to decide it's needed. You can even expose flows as MCP tools (covered below), getting the best of both worlds.

images.alt: MCP Servers page inside the Automation workspace
images.caption: Automation → MCP: the MCP Servers page, ready for your first server.

images.alt: MCP Servers page inside the Automation workspace
images.caption: Automation → MCP: the MCP Servers page, ready for your first server.

steps.title: Create and Name Your Server
steps.content:
1. Click + New MCP Server in the top-right.
  2. A new server is created instantly and you land on its page. It has two tabs: Configure (its tools) and History (every tool execution).
  3. Click the server name at the top (or the pencil icon next to it), type a descriptive name like Support Toolkit, and press Enter. The rename saves automatically.
💡 Tip: Name servers after the job they do — "Support Toolkit", "Sales Research", "Booking Actions". When you later attach servers to agents, a clear name beats "Untitled" every time.

images.alt: A new MCP server's Configure tab with no tools yet
images.caption: Your new server: the Configure tab starts empty — time to add tools.

images.alt: A new MCP server's Configure tab with no tools yet
images.caption: Your new server: the Configure tab starts empty — time to add tools.

steps.title: Add Tools: Two Sources
steps.content:
On the Configure tab, the Tools section is where the magic happens.

  1. Click Add tool on the right side of the Tools section.
  2. A small menu opens with two options:
  • From piece — pick ready-made actions from a catalog of 280+ integrations (Slack, Google Sheets, HubSpot, Stripe, plain HTTP requests, and many more).
  • From flow — expose one of your own automation flows as a tool, so the agent can trigger your custom logic on demand.

images.alt: Add tool menu with From piece and From flow options
images.caption: Add tool → choose From piece (app catalog) or From flow (your own flows).

images.alt: Add tool menu with From piece and From flow options
images.caption: Add tool → choose From piece (app catalog) or From flow (your own flows).

steps.title: From Piece: Pick an App
steps.content:
1. Click Add tool → From piece.
  2. The Add Tool dialog opens with a searchable grid of integrations.
  3. Type in the Search box to filter (e.g. slack, sheets, http).
  4. Click the app you want — the dialog switches to that app's list of available actions.
💡 Tip: The HTTP piece is the Swiss-army knife of the catalog: its Send HTTP request action lets your agent call any API on the internet, no dedicated integration required.

images.alt: Add Tool dialog with a searchable grid of app integrations
images.caption: The Add Tool dialog: search across 280+ app integrations.

images.alt: Add Tool dialog with a searchable grid of app integrations
images.caption: The Add Tool dialog: search across 280+ app integrations.

steps.title: Choose Actions and Save
steps.content:
1. Inside the app you picked, tick the checkbox next to each action you want the agent to have, or tick Select all.
  2. Click Save. The dialog closes and each selected action appears as its own tool on the server.
  3. Use the < back arrow at the top of the dialog if you want to pick a different app first, or Close to cancel.
⚠️ Important: Apps that talk to an account (Slack, Gmail, HubSpot…) show a Connection dropdown above the action list — the agent needs credentials to act on your behalf. Pick an existing connection or create one under Connections in the Automation mini-sidebar first. Simple pieces like HTTP need no connection at all.

images.alt: HTTP piece action list with Send HTTP request selected
images.caption: Tick the actions the agent may use, then click Save.

images.alt: HTTP piece action list with Send HTTP request selected
images.caption: Tick the actions the agent may use, then click Save.

steps.title: From Flow: Turn Your Automations into Tools
steps.content:
This is where MCP gets really powerful: any automation flow you've built can become a tool the agent calls on demand — multi-step logic, branching, database writes and all.

  1. Click Add tool → From flow.
  2. The Add Flow Tools dialog lists your eligible flows. Use Search flows to filter.
  3. Tick the flows you want, then click Save.
  4. No flows yet? Click the Create New Flow card to jump straight into the flow builder.
💡 Tip: If the dialog says No flows available, your flows may not be published yet or none exist — build and publish a flow under Automation → Flows first, then come back.

images.alt: Add Flow Tools dialog with search and Create New Flow card
images.caption: Add Flow Tools: expose your own flows as MCP tools.

images.alt: Add Flow Tools dialog with search and Create New Flow card
images.caption: Add Flow Tools: expose your own flows as MCP tools.

steps.title: Review the Tools on Your Server
steps.content:
1. Back on the Configure tab, every tool is listed as a card showing the app icon, the app name, and the technical action name (e.g. HTTP / send_request) — that action name is what the AI sees when deciding which tool to call.
  2. Keep stacking: click Add tool again to combine actions from many apps and flows on the same server — one server can be a complete department toolkit.

images.alt: MCP server Configure tab listing an HTTP send_request tool
images.caption: Each saved action shows up as its own tool card on the server.

images.alt: MCP server Configure tab listing an HTTP send_request tool
images.caption: Each saved action shows up as its own tool card on the server.

steps.title: Remove a Tool
steps.content:
1. On the tool's card, click the ⋮ (three-dot) menu on the right.
  2. Click Delete.
  3. A confirmation dialog asks "Are you sure you want to delete this tool?" — click Remove to confirm, or Cancel to keep it.
⚠️ Warning: Removing a tool takes it away from every agent using this server immediately. If an agent's script depends on that action, update the agent too.

images.alt: Delete tool confirmation dialog with Cancel and Remove buttons
images.caption: Deleting a tool asks for confirmation before removing it.

images.alt: Delete tool confirmation dialog with Cancel and Remove buttons
images.caption: Deleting a tool asks for confirmation before removing it.

steps.title: Manage Your Servers
steps.content:
1. Click the < back arrow next to the server name to return to the MCP Servers list.
  2. The table shows each server's Name, the icons of its Tools, and Last Modified. Click any row to open that server.
  3. To rename a server, open it and click the pencil icon next to its name.
  4. To delete servers, tick the checkbox at the left of one or more rows — a Delete button appears above the table. Confirm to remove them.
  5. Create as many servers as you need with + New MCP Server — for example one per team, one per agent persona, or one per customer workflow.

images.alt: MCP Servers list showing a server row with its tools
images.caption: The MCP Servers list: every server with its tools at a glance.

images.alt: MCP Servers list showing a server row with its tools
images.caption: The MCP Servers list: every server with its tools at a glance.

steps.title: Monitor Tool Usage with History
steps.content:
Once agents start using your server, you get a full audit trail.

  1. Open a server and click the History tab.
  2. The Tool Usage History table logs every execution with its Action, Status, and Created At time.
  3. Filter with the Action Name search or the Status filter to zero in on failures.
💡 Tip: Seeing No tool executions yet? That's normal until an agent actually calls a tool — come back after your first live test call.

images.alt: Tool Usage History tab with Action, Status and Created At columns
images.caption: The History tab tracks every tool execution your agents make.

images.alt: Tool Usage History tab with Action, Status and Created At columns
images.caption: The History tab tracks every tool execution your agents make.

steps.title: Put It to Work: Attach the Server to a Smart Agent
steps.content:
A toolbox only matters in the hands of a worker. The attach side lives in the agent editor:

  1. Go to Intelligence Center → Smart Agents and open an agent.
  2. In the agent's Tools / MCP panel, select your server from the MCP Server list — the agent instantly gains every tool on it.
  3. In the visual node editor you can go further: drop MCP Server nodes onto the canvas and wire them into specific Tasks, so different parts of the conversation get different toolkits. The node editor also accepts custom MCP servers — any external server by Server URL plus an optional auth header — so you can plug in MCP servers hosted outside iMash too.
  4. Then open Test Agent, connect, and ask for something that needs a tool — watch it happen, and watch the run land in your History tab.

The full attach walkthrough (including the custom-URL option) lives in the Agent MCP guide.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content:
If you run into any issues or have questions:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io
  • 💬 Join our Discord community
  • 📚 Check out our API Reference
  • 🎥 Watch our video tutorials

metaDescription: Create and manage MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers in iMash: register a server, add tools from 280+ app integrations or your own flows, monitor tool usage, and attach the server to your Smart Agents.
metaKeywords: mcp server, model context protocol, agent tools, integrations, automation, tool usage history, smart agent tools, from piece, from flow, imash
